title: Italy’s Best-Kept Wine Travel Secret Is a Quick Jaunt from Venice
summary:
After landing at Venice's Marco Polo Airport, why not head east instead? Postpone your gondola ride, rent a car, and cross into Friuli-Venezia Giulia, a lesser-visited region full of culinary gems.

The post Italy’s Best-Kept Wine Travel Secret Is a Quick Jaunt from Venice appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
When you land at Venice Marco Polo Airport, you instinctively want to hop on a water taxi and disappear into the magic of Venice. But what if you go east instead? I suggest you postpone your gondola ride, rent a car, and cross into Friuli-Venezia Giulia, a lesser-visited region full of culinary gems.

There are no anchor cities here, like Florence in Tuscany or Bologna in Emilia-Romagna. Instead, you’ll find quiet river towns, stretches of forest, hilly vineyards, and artisan families who have been making things for generations: knives, cheese, honey, prosciutto. Some stops on this Friuli itinerary feel like the edge of the known world. Others, like Trieste or Udine, are bustling cities where empires once clashed and cultures have been intertwining and negotiating for centuries.

“What excited me about Friuli [wine] was that everyone had a completely different take,” says Bobby Stuckey, Master Sommelier and co-founder of Frasca Food and Wine in Boulder, Colorado, who has been taking trips to the region for 30 years. “In Napa, you might find everyone working with the same consultant. In Friuli, everyone was doing something different, and they were all friends. That spirit never left.”

Sacile and San Daniele del Friuli

Your first stop, just inside Friuli’s western edge, is the town of Sacile. Walk along the river and order your first glass of Friulano, a dry white wine with citrus peel and almond notes. Nothing feels urgent, and that is the point.

To best understand Friuli, “Start with the people who make things,” says Elena Orzan, a local cultural ambassador. Head north to Maniago, where a knife- and blade-making tradition has continued for centuries and remains very much alive. Drive farther and discover the mosaics of Spilimbergo, assembled piece by piece over the centuries.

The road climbs toward San Daniele del Friuli, where La Bottega del Prosciutto di Levi, open since 1890, sells salumi, smoked ricotta from the Carnic Alps, local cheeses, and wines. Order prosciutto of different ages alongside a glass of Friulano from the tap and let the afternoon slow to a stop.

Before you leave, step into the Biblioteca Guarneriana, founded in 1466; its shelves hold 12,000 antique books and 600 manuscript codices, including a 14th-century handwritten copy of The Divine Comedy. Nearby, the Church of Sant’Antonio Abate is adorned with frescoes so stunning that it is known as the Sistine Chapel of Friuli.

WINE: Friulano Grave DOC, Pinot Grigio Ramato, Cividin

FOOD: Prosciutto di San Daniele, frico, ricotta affumicata

NOT TO MISS: Biblioteca Guarneriana, Church of Sant’Antonio Abate, Scuola Mosaicisti del Friuli

Tarcento, Savorgnano del Torre, and Nimis

Head into the foothills, where the land lifts and folds, vines appear in pockets framed by forest, and the mountains loom just beyond. In Tarcento, younger growers are returning to old vineyard sites and working with the Refosco dal Peduncolo Rosso grape with newfound precision. A short drive brings you to the village of Savorgnano del Torre, where the wines lean on Friulano and Picolit, which is more famous as a sweet variety, but here it’s dry, driven, fresh, and electric.

Continue higher to Nimis, home to the sweet wine Ramandolo, one of Friuli’s quiet revelations that’s defined by its structure, tension, and a deep connection to the land. Stop at the winery Dario Coos for a tasting that reframes everything you thought you knew about the variety.

“Friuli’s strength is that no one is trying to make the same wine,” says Lachlan Mackinnon-Patterson, co-founder of Frasca Food and Wine and Benvenusa Imports. “That independence is what keeps it alive.”

WINE: Ramandolo DOCG (Verduzzo), Savorgnano del Torre Bianco, Refosco dal Peduncolo Rosso

FOOD: pitina, Prosciutto di Sauris, strucolo de pomi

NOT TO MISS: Castello di Tarcento, Valli del Torre, Castello di Coia

Udine

In Udine’s architecture, you see Venice in the façades, but sense something else in the buildings themselves—Habsburg order and spacing, a rhythm that’s distinctly Central European. Walk up to the castle for a view that reveals the whole region: the plains stretching west, the hills rising to the east, the mountains cutting the northern horizon.

Come down into the Palazzo Arcivescovile, where Tiepolo painted some of his most luminous early frescoes. Then find a table. The real magic of Udine happens at street level, in the aperitivo hour that begins around 5 p.m. and keeps no fixed schedule. A glass of Friulano, a crostino, a conversation already in progress. This is not performance; this is how the city works.

On Saturdays, the spot to hit is Osteria al Cappello. Stuckey says it’s essential, the kind of restaurant where you immediately feel part of city life, rather than watching it from the outside.

WINE: Pinot Bianco, Malvasia Istriana, Tazzelenghe

FOOD: frico, crostini con lardo, musetto con brovada

NOT TO MISS: Casa Cavazzini modern and contemporary art museum, Castello di Udine, Piazza Libertà

Cividale del Friuli

Cividale was founded as Forum Julii, possibly by Julius Caesar, around 50 B.C.E., and later became the first capital of the Lombard duchy, giving the entire region its name. The Ponte del Diavolo bridge spans the gorge with drama that stops you in your tracks. The Tempietto Longobardo, an eighth-century chapel tucked into the riverbank, features carved figures that have survived intact for 13 centuries. Cividale is not a footnote; it is where the story of Friuli begins.

Find a table for cjarsons, the local stuffed pasta filled with herbs, ricotta, and sometimes a touch of chocolate or cinnamon. It’s a recipe that’s been carried across the centuries and made differently in every kitchen.

WINE: Schioppettino di Prepotto, Rosazzo Bianco, Pignolo

FOOD: cjarsons, gubana, trota del Natisone

NOT TO MISS: Piazza Paolo Diacono, Duomo di Santa Maria Assunta, Rosazzo Abbey

Cormòns, Collio, and Oslavia

Head south toward Cormòns and into Collio, where you’ll be greeted by vines in every direction and soft light. As you move through the landscape, the place names shift and the language on the signs softens. Without realizing it, you have crossed into Brda, Slovenia. Producers like Edi Keber work on both sides of the border, not as a statement, but because it has always been this way.

“The diversity within such a small stamp of land is hard to describe,” says Giampaolo Venica of the winery Venica and Venica, which is known for its single-vineyard Sauvignon Blanc. Friulian Sauvignon Blanc sits between the minerality and herbal notes of great Sancerre and the punchy fruit of New Zealand Sauvignon Blanc—a mix of crushed stone, fresh herbs, and white grapefruit with an acidity that defines what “racy” means in wine.

Stay at La Subida, an eco-friendly resort run by the Sirk family. Polenta arrives from a copper pot, churned for hours and cut by a string as tradition demands. Visits turn into conversations, conversations turn into corks being pulled, and lifelong friendships are made. “They don’t have the pressure of tourism,” says Bobby Stuckey of the region. “They are genuinely excited that you are there.”

Then find your way to Oslavia, 200 meters from the Slovenian border, where a war memorial above the vines holds the remains of more than 57,000 soldiers who fell here in World War I. What grew back was wine. The village has seven producers, including Gravner, Radikon, Fiegl, and Dario Prinčič. In the late 1990s, Gravner and Radikon returned to ancient techniques, skin maceration, and amphoras buried in the earth. What the world called orange wine was, in Oslavia, simply a return to their legacy.

WINE: Collio Bianco, Ribolla Gialla macerata, Collio Rosso

FOOD: polenta tagliata, D’Osvaldo prosciutto, frtalja

NOT TO MISS: Sacrario Militare di Oslavia, La Subida, Route of the Orange Benches

Gorizia and Carso

Gorizia tells its story without raising its voice. For decades, it was two cities: Gorizia in Italy and Nova Gorica in what was then Yugoslavia (now Slovenia), with a wall dividing them during the Cold War. Today, you can walk across Piazza della Transalpina, a shared square that straddles the border.

Continue south into Carso, or Kras, where limestone dominates the thin soil and rock must be broken to give the vines a chance. What comes back are wines of clarity and tension: white Vitovska and red Terrano. Find an osmiza, a temporary farm tavern marked by a branch outside when it’s open, like the one at Zidarich winery, and sit down at a long table for wine, salumi, and cheese. “The real magic of Friuli isn’t found in its landmarks,” says Isabella Lupi of Amandum winery. “It’s in people’s homes, around the table.”

WINE: Vitovska, Terrano del Carso

FOOD: jota (or iota), goulash alla goriziana, Kraški pršut

NOT TO MISS: Piazza della Transalpina, Castello di Gorizia, Grotta Gigante

Trieste

Finally, you arrive in Trieste, and everything converges. It was once the Habsburg Empire’s great Adriatic port, a crossroads where merchants, sailors, writers, and refugees coexisted. The café culture they cultivated remains vibrant, from Caffè degli Specchi and Caffè San Marco to the old Viennese-style coffeehouses that line the main avenue, each with its own regulars and rituals. In Trieste, a café is not a stop on the way to somewhere else; it is the destination.

The novelist Italo Svevo, born and buried here, gave the city its literary soul. Its identity has never quite settled, with history always pressing in and beauty and melancholy appearing as the same thing seen from different angles. James Joyce felt it, too, living here for many years. Two of the 20th century’s great literary minds independently chose to live in the same small city on the edge of a crumbling empire, so something special was clearly happening there. Stand in Piazza Unità d’Italia, which opens onto the Adriatic, and you’ll understand it.

The food scene is as layered as the history; don’t miss the storied buffets where pork cuts have been carved and served by weight for over a century. Go to Karaktér for seafood and natural wine and to Pagna Panificio Artigianale for sourdough and a bottle list that spans the Old and New Worlds.

Walking through Trieste, you begin to recognize everything you have experienced in the region: Slovenian influence, Italian pace, the weight of history, the openness of the sea. This is the sum of Friuli-Venezia Giulia. When people leave, they ask the same question: Why don’t I live here? Friuli doesn’t offer a good answer. It just waits for you to return.

WINE: Malvasia del Carso, Terrano Rosato, Carso Bianco

FOOD: bollito di maiale con kren, sardoni in savor, putizza

NOT TO MISS: Miramare Castle, Piazza Unità d’Italia, Museo Revoltella

title: ICYMI: Smoked Dessert Wine Exists, and It’s Great
summary:
Umbrian farmers are reviving vinosanto da uva affumicata, a smoked dessert wine that was once on the brink of extinction.

The post ICYMI: Smoked Dessert Wine Exists, and It’s Great appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
The dogs are going crazy.

They trot behind Chiara Filippi, greeting the stranger who has just arrived. Filippi shakes my hand and introduces her husband, Claudio Ceccarelli, who has just returned from the forest with truffles dug up by their three yellow labs.

It’s an early-November morning in Umbria. Mist clings to the golden leaves that blanket the region’s rolling hills, like wisps of smoke rising from the valley. It’s the offseason, but there’s no downtime at an agriturismo like La Miniera di Galparino. Even if Filippi’s rooms aren’t booked, there’s work to be done on the farm: feeding the chickens, tending the olive groves, and checking on the grapes hanging above the hearth.

Since 2000, Filippi and Ceccarelli have run this 120-acre farmstay in the Upper Tiber Valley, near the historic walled city of Città di Castello. For even longer, they’ve been producing vinosanto da uva affumicata, a sweet wine made from smoked grapes, carrying on a tradition passed down by Filippi’s father. You’ll only find this wine here, in the heart of Italy’s tobacco country.

I was curious how smoke, something usually considered a flaw, could be a defining characteristic of their wine. After five minutes on the farm—seeing the vines of a nearly extinct grape variety, the willow branches they use to tie grape bunches, and the roses planted in the vineyard to be an early warning for disease—I started to see the bigger picture.

When Filippi and Ceccarelli began bottling it, vinosanto da uva affumicata had nearly disappeared. It was too time consuming to make and impossible to scale. La Miniera di Galparino’s is a blend of Trebbiano Toscano and three other local varieties, and they only produce about 150 bottles a year. But thanks to the nonprofit organization Slow Food, 11 families in the valley are receiving support to keep producing it, even if the commercial prospects are practically non-existent. Now, this tradition, tied to the region’s tobacco industry, has a future that seemed uncertain until recently.

Tobacco Fields and Kitchen Fires

Italy is Europe’s largest tobacco producer, and Umbria is one of the country’s leading sources. For about 450 years, not long after it was brought to Europe from the Americas, tobacco has been grown, harvested, and cured here, especially around Città di Castello. Driving around the region with Sergio Consigli, the head of Slow Food Umbria, the crop’s staggering value becomes clear. At one point, Consigli starts crying out, “Bancomat! [ATM]” every time we pass a tobacco field. I lose count of how many times he says it.

Historically, though, Umbrian tobacco farmers were not wealthy. Most had to dry tobacco in their farmhouses. In the same spaces, they also produced vinosanto (“holy wine”), traditionally made by drying grapes for months until they become raisins.

Without another room to devote solely to winemaking, Umbrian families would hang tobacco leaves and grapes together above their kitchen hearths. “They were peasants. They had to make use of whatever space they had,” Filippi tells me.

As they dried, the grapes would absorb the aroma of the tobacco leaves, as well as what Consigli calls arrosto, the smoke of whatever was cooked in the kitchen. The process yielded a wine unlike the vinosanto made on the other side of the hills in Tuscany. “This,” says Consigli, a proud Umbrian, “is only here—nowhere else on Earth.”

The Taste of Smoke

With Slow Food’s assistance, the production of vinosanto da uva affumicata has now been codified. The grapes must be strung up and naturally influenced by smoke, and the wine must age in barrels no bigger than 100 liters for at least five years.

Under the direction of Filippi and Ceccarelli’s son, Nicola, La Miniera di Galparino ages their wines for 10 years. The extra time gives them a character unlike any I’ve tasted.

Each of their wines is sweet and has a rich mahogany color. Before I taste it, Consigli instructs me to shut my eyes, cup my hands around the glass, and draw a deep breath. “After that, you’ll tell me, ‘It’s amazing!’ and I’ll say, ‘I know,’” he says. Whiffs of peat and tobacco layer with the fig, honey, and salted caramel flavors on my palate. Then we open a 20-year-old bottle. Its flavor is so profound that even Ceccarelli, who helped make the stuff, is nearly brought to tears.

After the tasting, I’m led to an old stable the couple converted into a kitchen for guests. Dangling from the rafters are the coppiole (couples), the neatly tied bunches of grapes that make up the wine. About 80% of it is Trebbiano; the rest is a mix of Dolciame Bianco, Grechetto, and Malfiore, a variety that was on the verge of extinction when they found it growing on the property. The family is now helping to revive it with support from the University of Perugia.

“Vinosanto grapes are always picked first,” Ceccarelli explains. “They need the right amount of space between the fruit so that air passes through and they don’t mold.”

After harvest, tradition dictates you tie them up with willow branches. In January or February, after pressing, the must is then put in barrels with the most important ingredient, the mother yeast, which stirs back into action when the weather warms up in spring and summer.

Like sourdough starters, each winemaking family here has its own mother yeast. Some, like La Miniera di Galparino’s, are more than 100 years old. “The mother is everything to vinosanto,” says Filippi. “In it, you taste the flavors of the seasons and a sense of the past.”

A Bottle at Birth, a Bottle at Marriage

After the tasting, I drive across the valley to meet farmer Davide Morganti. He calls his vinosanto Bubbola after the hoopoe, a migratory bird seen as a harbinger of spring. Morganti releases about 500 bottles a year, selling many to local restaurants.

He says making wine is as much an excuse to be with his family as it is to make money. After 15 years in a factory, Morganti came home 25 years ago to make wine with his father. His brother designs the labels. In their free time, they hunt for truffles and wild asparagus. “It’s a better life,” he tells me.

“Every farmer here has made vinosanto at least once,” Morganti explains as we savor a 10-year-old wine that smells just like the Umbrian countryside in autumn. “It’s a drink for celebrations.”

In a time before tractors, farmers would treat their neighbors to vinosanto and cake in return for helping bring their harvest to market. “We also make vinosanto the year our children are born. When they get married, we drink that year’s bottle,” he adds. “Vinosanto is the identity of this place. It’s important we keep making it as best we can.”

Back at La Miniera di Galparino, over a meal of steak drizzled with homemade olive oil and risotto with wild porcini mushrooms, the wine’s role in the connective tissue of the valley becomes even clearer. “Vinosanto was born because poor people wanted to have a precious thing to give to guests,” Filippi says. “We were poor people making something rich.”

They’re not peasant farmers anymore, but that instinct to turn what they have into something worth sharing hasn’t gone anywhere. In today’s world, where everything has been optimized, she believes a wine like this can still be a gesture of hospitality and a regional icon.

“This used to be a place you’d pass through,” Filippi says. The resurgence of vinosanto might give the valley a flagship product—and with it, she says, “valore, some value.”

title: Italy’s Smallest Wine Region Is a Valley Frozen in Time
summary:
Spanning just 450 hectares, Valle d'Aosta is rife with old-world charm and brisk alpine wines.

The post Italy’s Smallest Wine Region Is a Valley Frozen in Time appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
I stood in my Keds on the edge of a slushy snowbank, 8,500 feet up in the Italian Alps. A hundred yards away, a refuge hut with a chimney stack promised warmth and lunch. To say I was ill-prepared for this journey would be an understatement.

Crossing snowy peaks hadn’t been the plan when I left the hotel that morning. It was 75 degrees where we were standing. The receptionist said the trails were easy, well manicured, and fit for all ages. We’d reach the waterfalls within 45 minutes and then have lunch at the refuge hut. I thought my travel partner, Anthony, and I were taking a quick walk along a picturesque path through swaying grasses dotted with edelweiss blossoms. Nobody mentioned snow, proper footwear, or a jacket for my bare shoulders. All I packed was a bottle of Elio Ottin’s Fumin and some mocetta, the local cured beef.

As fast-walking New Yorkers, we blew through the waterfalls in half the suggested time. Pushing all the way to lunch now seemed like a no-brainer—until we saw backpackers carrying their skis up the switchback trail ahead of us. The air was thin, my back was sunburned, and Anthony disappeared ahead. Wild rams with curled horns stared me down from across the valley, daring me to keep going.

Two days earlier, we’d driven north from Piedmont into Valle d’Aosta, Italy’s smallest wine region. We followed the Dora Baltea river through the ancient Saint Bernard pass, the most important Alpine crossroad between Southern and Central Europe since pre-Roman times. Even traversing a modern highway felt like being shuttled to not just another place, but another time.

We passed medieval castles perched along bluffs, built by whomever controlled the valley at the time. Nestled between Europe’s highest peaks—Mont Blanc, Monte Rosa, and the Matterhorn—terraced vineyards and orchards were etched into the mountainsides. The scene was straight out of The Sound of Music.

We rolled into the town of Cogne as the sunset lit the jagged peaks above the Bellevue Hotel and Spa. The resort was rife with Old-World charm: herb gardens for the in-house restaurant, Renaissance frescoes on the lobby ceilings, antique furniture, and knick-knacks everywhere. Dinner was gooey raclette over potatoes and local steaks, paired beautifully with Ermes Pavese Blanc de Morgex et de la Salle—a white so brisk it could have come straight from glacier melt.

Wine Country, Alpine-Style

Valle d’Aosta is a tiny wine region—about 450 hectares planted, the same size as Zuccardi’s Santa Julia vineyard in Mendoza—and in some ways, Italy’s most versatile. The region’s wines had almost disappeared, only to be revived in the 1970s with a whopping 19 grape varieties, something to suit everyone.

There are a few you’ve heard of, like Gamay, Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, and Pinot Gris. The region’s Nebbiolo, locally called Picotendro, may be the original source of the more famous Piedmont wines. But there are many varieties you won’t find anywhere else and probably aren’t familiar with. The regal Petite Arvine is an excellent substitute for Chardonnay, Cornalin is angular and structured, and Mayolet is perfumed and silky.

Our timing was perfect: It was an open-cave day, so most wineries were welcoming visitors. Grosjean Vins, one of the larger family-owned estates, specializes in growing and bottling most of the indigenous varieties separately. With alpine freshness and mineral purity as a backbone, they are among the few sources exploring the region’s grapes side by side. Down the valley at the La Kiuva cooperative, with seven or eight families farming plots as small as 200 square meters, we tried their Nebbiolo-based red blend—light bodied, aromatic, fruity, and age-worthy.

Valle d’Aosta Grape Varieties

Petit Rouge

The “little red one” is indigenous to Valle d’Aosta and its most popular grape. It is occasionally labeled varietally, although it makes up the bulk of blends like Torrette and Enfer d’Arvier. The wines are full of juicy red-berry flavors, rich in body, and velvety in tannins.

Fumin

Bloody, sanguine wines with inky black juice and meaty, herbal flavors akin to Syrah from the Northern Rhône. They have astute tannins and a core of acidity that keeps them buoyant and fresh.

Prié Blanc

Also known as Blanc de Morgex, this grape soars to great heights, growing at elevations of nearly 4,000 feet—the highest in mainland Europe. The wines are airy and mineral, with bright acidity, making them great for sparkling wine.

Cornalin

Saved from extinction in the 1980s, this finicky grape makes mountain wine with notes of cracked pepper, tart berries, and wild herbs. With low tannin and laser acidity, it’s a delicate red, great served slightly chilled.

Mayolet

Tightly packed clusters make this grape difficult to grow, and it is rarely bottled on its own. Those who put in the effort are richly rewarded with intense aromatics of rose petals, mountain herbs, and sweet incense, along with juicy berry flavors and soft, silky tannins.

What Grows Together

Italy markets itself as an agriturismo experience, but most farms in the country are monocultures. Valle d’Aosta is different, with agricultural versatility being a defining feature. Only 1% of vineyards are bigger than a hectare. Crushed against mountainsides, mechanization is impossible. Farmers work by hand, forced to diversify to survive—grains, potatoes, and livestock can be found alongside the vines.

The old town of Aosta, still surrounded by ancient Roman walls, gets surprisingly hot daytime temperatures during the summer. Locals cultivate both Alpine and warm-climate fare—almonds, figs, and pistachios—alongside herbs like génépy, savory, and thyme, as well as cheese, apples, meat, and grains.

This creates the ultimate “what grows together, goes together” experience. Whites are paired with wild boar, reds alongside river fish. Fontina, aged in Alpine herbs, loves a dry, aromatic Muscat. In restaurants decorated with handmade plaid curtains, you’ll eat fresh-baked bread from local grains, potatoes in every form, meat they raised or hunted, all washed down with valley wines. A glass of génépy liqueur from a home still often follows, all served with genuine mountain hospitality.

Back on the Mountain

Standing at that snow bank, my stomach growled and my back burned. My Keds were soaked through. As uncomfortable as it would be to keep walking, I had to make it to the hut.

Once inside, we popped the Fumin. It was sanguine and meaty, with notes of alpine herbs and violets, lean acidity, and velvety tannins. After that trek, anything would’ve been welcome, but this was divine with our rustic pasta ragù.

Our socks dried by the woodstove. We polished off the wine, tied our shoes, and stepped back into the snow. We made it down the mountain with wet feet, singing the entire way.

title: As Other Spirits Struggle, Soju Is Winning Over U.S. Drinkers
summary:
As the spirit moves into American stadiums, cocktail bars, and people’s homes, soju is no longer just a marker of Korean identity.

The post As Other Spirits Struggle, Soju Is Winning Over U.S. Drinkers appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Growing up Korean American, I associated soju with two settings: K-barbecue restaurants and rowdy house hangs with fellow Koreans. In recent years, though, I’ve started seeing it everywhere.

In Winooski, Vermont’s smallest city, I stumbled upon Donwoori, a homey restaurant serving excellent Korean-style fried chicken and soju cocktails, including a creamy-sweet one made with a Melona ice cream bar, another Korean convenience store staple. 

At the New York City trattoria Sono, chef Sechul Yang is experimenting with house-infused soju—flavored with Korean pantry ingredients like ginseng, bamboo, and plum—to pair with his imaginative Italian-Korean fare, including rigatoni studded with guanciale and fermented black beans. Across the country, at Los Angeles’ BMO Stadium, soccer fans sip Hello Soju’s sparkling canned drinks while cheering on Korean superstar Son Heung-min.

Soju’s rise in the United States occurs at the intersection of several broader trends: the desire for lighter, more affordable drinks, the shift in generational drinking habits, and the Korean cultural boom.

Last year, Drinks International, a United Kingdom-based trade publication covering the global drinks industry, named Jinro the world’s best-selling spirit brand for 2024. The brand has held the title for 24 consecutive years, underscoring soju’s growing global appeal, especially in the U.S. 

According to Hwang Jung-ho, executive vice president of HiteJinro’s Overseas Business Division, the company’s exports to the U.S. “recorded a compound annual growth rate of approximately 28% from 2020 to 2025,” reflecting what he describes as “solid and sustained growth.” Meanwhile, HiteJinro continues to roll out its products across major retail chains such as Albertsons, Costco, and Target.

High-profile partnerships and marketing campaigns have only bolstered soju’s ubiquity. Hello Soju recently became LAFC’s official RTD partner, while Jinro is an official sponsor of both the Los Angeles Dodgers and the New York Red Bulls. Last summer, Jinro also served as the exclusive alcohol partner for a Squid Game Season 3 fan event at Brooklyn’s Domino Park.

Soju’s ascent is especially notable at a moment when overall alcohol consumption in America is declining. While many spirit brands are struggling to retain drinkers, soju appears to be expanding the category by attracting consumers who might otherwise avoid hard liquor.

Reinventing a Classic

Though often dismissed as cheap and easy to drink, soju has a complex history dating back to the 13th-century Goryeo dynasty, when Mongol invaders under Genghis Khan introduced distillation techniques for arak, which they had learned from the Persians. Koreans later adapted these methods to make rice-based soju.

During the South Korean rice shortages in the 1960s, the government imposed a ban on the traditional distillation of soju from rice. Eventually, this resulted in what most of us know today as soju: green glass bottles filled with diluted ethanol distilled from sweet potatoes, tapioca, and wheat. 

Following the ban’s lift in 1999, craft soju brands slowly began popping up, with Tokki Soju among them. “We felt the category needed better representation, especially in terms of classification and quality,” says Douglas Kim, who co-owns the brand with master distiller Brandon Hill. “We wanted to offer a genuine alternative: a historically accurate, rice-based soju made the traditional way, without the shortcuts.” 

Tokki Soju’s portfolio includes five types of soju, including the barrel-aged Tokki Soju Gold Label, and is poured at some of the country’s most notable cocktail destinations, like New York City’s The Portrait Bar and The Wellesbourne in Los Angeles. 

What’s changing now is not soju itself, but who’s drinking it—and how. As the spirit moves into stadiums, cocktail bars, and perhaps most importantly, people’s homes, it’s no longer just a marker of Korean identity. It’s becoming a part of the broader American drinking culture.

Affordable, Adaptable, and Communal

Soju’s clarity, crisp bite, and subtly sweet finish have also played into its recent popularity as a cocktail darling. According to Irene Yoo, owner of Orion Bar and author of the 2026 James Beard Media Awards–winning Soju Party, soju’s adaptability is one of its greatest strengths. 

Though traditionally consumed neat with formal drinking rituals, soju has increasingly become a base for inventive drinks, including subak soju (a punch served in a hollowed-out watermelon) and tangy, creamy yakult soju, both featured in Soju Party. “It’s honestly just a really fun party drink,” she says. “It’s a great way for people to partake in a unique part of Korean culture, try something new, and share with their friends.” 

And at 16% to 20% abv across commercial brands, soju attracts drinkers—especially Gen Z consumers—seeking lower-abv options compared to vodka or whiskey.

In contrast to more casual American drinking norms—where socializing often centers on the drinks themselves—Koreans almost always drink with food. Known as anju, these dishes—common examples include savory pancakes, fried chicken, and kimchi stew—tend to be rich and carb-heavy. More importantly, anju is meant to be shared communally, much like soju itself, and is believed to slow the effects of alcohol. (The word anju roughly translates to food that “keeps the alcohol down.”)

As the cost of going out continues to climb, along with the prices of imported wines and spirits, mass-produced soju remains relatively affordable. A standard 375-milliliter bottle of Jinro Soju currently costs between $5 and $8 at most retail liquor stores. Although restaurants and bars naturally mark up bottles above wholesale cost, soju remains well within reach for most drinkers.

title: Once Only Sold in Mexico, Still-Strength Tequila Is Taking Over the U.S.
summary:
Totally undiluted, still-strength tequila delivers unparalleled flavor and a whoppingly high abv.

The post Once Only Sold in Mexico, Still-Strength Tequila Is Taking Over the U.S. appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
There was a time when tequila just tasted different in Mexico. Not because it was consumed in the spirit’s homeland. Or because it could be sipped within feet of an agave field or alongside the distiller who crafted it with their own hands. It’s because for many years, the only way consumers could taste the unadulterated flavor of still-strength tequila was to buy a plane ticket and visit a distillery.

Luckily, that’s no longer the case. While most tequila clocks in at 40% abv (or 80 proof), distillers are now opting to bottle product straight off the stills. Still-strength tequila is a totally undiluted version of the liquid that increases the abv as high as 110, delivering the most robust version of their product to your glass.

From big names like Patrón, which put out its first ever still-strength bottle last month, to artisanal producers like Fortaleza and Cascahuín, more brands than ever are offering this high-proof spirit. And these bold, expressive bottlings are the latest obsession of agave fans and bartenders.

Still-Strength Tequila: Higher Abv, Richer Aromas

Once agave plants are cooked, shredded, fermented, and distilled to make blanco tequila, water is typically added to the final liquid until it reaches the desired abv level. But when distillers opt to forgo the dilution process and bottle up tequila exactly as it comes off copper pot-stills, it’s called still-strength tequila. 

For most producers, liquid comes off the still at about 92 to 110 proof, with 110 proof being the legal limit for tequila being sold in the United States. That means still-strength tequilas range from 46% to 55% abv, a significant boost from the typical offering of 40% abv.

When tequila is bumped up to a higher abv, all of the flavor that’s instilled in the liquid is dialed up a notch. Robert Nieves, the northeast market manager for Volans Tequila, describes it as “taking the flavor notes of your spirit and putting them on steroids.” 

“If there are grapefruit notes in your blanco, for example, you’re gonna taste a more intense flavor, like grapefruit rind, instead of just the essence of grapefruit,” Nieves says. While still-strength expressions primarily boost flavors that would also be detectable at lower abvs, new tasting notes also tend to emerge once more alcohol is there to amplify them.

“It’s important to realize that proof is a flavoring agent,” Nieves says. “Something at 90 proof versus something at 92 proof versus something at 80 proof are all going to taste completely different.”

Since every distillery has a different approach to crafting tequila, the flavors of still-strengths vary across the industry. Volans Tequila takes the added step to ferment its 106-proof expression with agave fibers to dial up the minerality and show off the flavor of its high-quality highland agave. Other brands lean into rich sweetness, as Tequila Ocho does for its limited-edition Plata Puntas bottling, while the beloved workhorse bottle of Tapatio 110 and small-batch Cazcanes No. 10 are known for savory peppery notes.

Like cask-strength bourbon and other barrel-proof spirits, still-strength tequila is considered the most honest expression of the spirit. Nieves calls it “a way to flex your production process” because it’s tough to hide impurities or low-quality ingredients at such a high proof.

He adds that when distillers are thoughtful about production, a higher abv can also lead to a richer, more viscous mouthfeel. “People tend to think high-proof spirits will be harsh or abrasive, but I actually think that a lot of still strength is very delicate in nature,” Nieves says. “You’re taking something that should be a punch in the mouth and making it a really nuanced experience.”

The Rise of Still-Strength Tequila in the U.S.

While this liquid was always available to consume at distilleries and around Mexico, still-strength tequila was rarely exported to the U.S. until recently—and it’s never been available as widely as it is today. 

Don Fulano launched one of the first still-strength tequilas available in the U.S. in 2002, and its 100-proof Fuerte is still a favorite among agave fans. However, Fortaleza is actually credited with popularizing the term “still-strength” on the bottle, as its 92-proof expression has a cult following. 

Small, artisanal distillers were once the primary bottlers of still-strength tequila because undiluted spirit is extremely expensive to produce. Now as stronger expressions are embraced, major brands are getting in on the action with LALO, Casa Cuervo’s 1800 Tequila, and Patrón all launching their own still-strength bottlings within the last year.

The ever-growing appetite for agave in the U.S. and increasing availability means still-strength tequila is finding its way behind more of the country’s best bars.

David Rodríguez, Patrón’s master distiller, says he noticed that “as knowledge of the tequila category has deepened, bartenders have increasingly sought out tequilas with greater depth, character, and a more agave-forward profile”—and those are all characteristics that only higher-proof tequila can deliver.

Bartenders are embracing still-strength tequila because it’s incredibly versatile. “Having a 100-proof bottle of excellent tequila gives us greater flexibility for a greater range of cocktail applications,” says Sam Nellis, head bartender at Silver Lyan in Washington, D.C. 

Shaken up in a margarita or Paloma, still-strength tequila holds its own and lets the agave flavor shine through even against strong citrus notes. But it’s just as tasty on the rocks or served neat, making it the perfect style for exploring all the flavor possibilities that agave has to offer.

title: Best Winery Restaurants 2026
summary:
We never say no to a cheeseboard. But when you want to pair your tasting-room visit with a memorable meal, book a table at one of these 57 destination restaurants. By Nils Bernstein At their best, winery restaurants do more than just feed tasting-room guests. They mirror the winery itself: obsessive about place, and determined

The post Best Winery Restaurants 2026 appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
We never say no to a cheeseboard. But when you want to pair your tasting-room visit with a memorable meal, book a table at one of these 57 destination restaurants.

By Nils Bernstein

At their best, winery restaurants do more than just feed tasting-room guests.

They mirror the winery itself: obsessive about place, and determined to say something distinct. That ambition can be surprisingly difficult to pull off, since in much of the world ( especially the U.S.), restrictions around agricultural land and alcohol production make running full-service restaurants at wineries difficult. Furthermore, the winery itself needs to be worth a visit, since you can’t have a great winery restaurant without great wine.

This list celebrates our favorite winery restaurants around the globe, from casual bites overlooking vineyards to elaborate tasting menus that rank among the world’s best. These aren’t just spots for a meat-and-cheese platter between pours. They are destinations to plan trips around and gain a deeper understanding of the wine regions they call home.

ANGÉLICA COCINA MAESTRA

Bodega Catena Zapata

Mendoza, Argentina

Angélica Cocina Maestra turns dining into an exploration of Mendoza’s high-altitude farms and vineyards. Located at Catena Zapata’s pyramid-shaped winery, the restaurant structures tasting menus around specific vineyard elevations and historic vintages. Against the dramatic backdrop of the Andes, Chefs Josefina Diana and Juan Manuel Feijoo rework Argentine traditions, pairing veal sweetbreads with beets, or the slender local pejerrey fish with krein, a horseradish condiment brought by Jewish immigrants.

MARIS RESTÓ

Bodega Roberto Bonfanti

Mendoza, Argentina

For an inland region, Mendoza has exceptional seafood, flown in daily from the coasts. Here, it pairs beautifully with the estate’s wines, a refreshing departure from the area’s usual lineup of beef parrilladas with Malbec and Cabernet. The familial hospitality is what you’d expect from a longstanding family winery, and views of century-old olive trees add to the romance.

RICCITELLI BISTRÓ

Matías Riccitelli Wines

Mendoza, Argentina

Riccitelli Bistró sits among old vines in Las Compuertas, one of Mendoza’s highest and coolest vineyard areas, and the setting shapes much of the experience. Chef Juan Ventureyra cooks nearly everything over an open fire, using produce from the restaurant’s organic garden. The rustic atmosphere fits naturally with Matías Riccitelli’s low-intervention wines and the mountain-framed vineyard landscape.

ROSELL BOHER LODGE

Rosell Boher

Mendoza, Argentina

Long lunches here regularly stretch into the afternoon beside the outdoor pool, usually accompanied by one of Rosell Boher’s traditional-method sparkling wines. Dinner guests get a cozier vibe and an exploration of local and seasonal ingredients, with both mountain and vineyard views.

SAVIA COCINA

Casarena Bodega y Vinedos

Mendoza, Argentina

Mendoza’s slow-paced hospitality is epitomized by the refined-but-not-fussy cuisine at Savia Cocina. Overlooking vineyards in Luján de Cuyo, some of which are nearing a century old, the restaurant centers on open-fire cooking, seasonal produce, and Argentine comfort dishes designed for extended lunches. Mountain views, shaded terraces, and Malbec pairings give the experience an easygoing, celebratory rhythm.

Australia

Back to Top

THE LANE VINEYARD RESTAURANT

The Lane Vineyard

Adelaide Hills, Australia

About 20 minutes from Adelaide, The Lane offers unparalleled views of the Onkaparinga Valley. The menu serves inventive takes on Southern Australian ingredients; a recent meal started with prawn with lemon myrtle, pumpkin with marigold, and turnip with salmon roe. If they have it, order their own vineyard-trodding lamb paired with their famous Syrah.

WILLS DOMAIN

Wills Domain Winery

Margaret River, Australia

Wills Domain has long been one of Margaret River’s standout dining destinations, partly because of its sweeping Gunyulgup Valley views, but mostly for modern Australian menus built around local seafood, produce, and Southwest ingredients. Start with rock oyster with finger lime; end with the winery’s own honey served with native desert lime and quandong fruits, and locally grown mango and ginger.

Austria

Back to Top

SATTLERHOF

Weingut Sattlerhof

Südsteiermark, Austria

Sattlerhof is one of Austria’s leading wine estates, and its Michelin-starred restaurant is set among the rolling hills of the Südsteiermark region, near the Slovenian border. The kitchen highlights seasonal Austrian ingredients prepared with delicacy and precision, like pikeperch with just-shelled peas and venison with foraged morels.

Brazil

Back to Top

ARENITO

Vinícola Uvva

Chapada Diamantina, Brazil

Arenito stands out in part because of its location in Brazil’s Chapada Diamantina, a high-altitude region historically better known for mining than for wine. The restaurant leans into the breadth of Brazilian cuisine, from the traditional (cod with olives and eggs) to the modern (sun-dried local lamb with plantain purée and broccoli foam). The architecture is strikingly contemporary, making the contrast with the rugged surroundings even more dramatic.

Canada

Back to Top

RESTAURANT PEARL MORISSETTE

Pearl Morissette Winery

Niagara Peninsula, Canada

The restaurant at Pearl Morissette Winery has evolved from a modest expression of the region’s farms to one of North America’s most acclaimed and influential restaurants. With a philosophy that both wine and food should, above all, express a sense of place, the leisurely tasting menu focuses on Canadian producers that share this mindset. Namely: a birch and apple custard tart with woodruff ice cream and the coffee-like chaga mushroom that grows on birch trees in eastern Canada’s chilly climate.

OLD VINES RESTAURANT

Quails’ Gate Winery

Okanagan Valley, Canada

Nearly every table here comes with a view of Okanagan Lake, a perfect setting for British Columbian dishes like white king salmon cured with the cylindrical, tart-sweet blue haskap berry or lamb chops with a spruce-tip jus. Dishes are designed to pair with the wines, which include Riesling, Chenin Blanc, Syrah, and Merlot, as well as the fresh and expressive Chardonnays and Pinot Noirs the estate is known for.

Chile

Back to Top

RAYUELA WINE & GRILL

Viu Manent

Colchagua, Chile

Rayuela at Viu Manent captures the more relaxed side of Colchagua wine country. The restaurant is known for clay-oven cooking, Chilean comfort dishes, and outdoor tables surrounded by vines and horse paddocks. Guests often arrive after horseback rides or carriage tours of the estate, which lends the whole place an easygoing atmosphere rooted in the rhythm of the winery itself.

VIK ZERO

VIK Wine

Cachapoal Valley, Chile

VIK Zero feels closer to a contemporary architectural project than a conventional winery restaurant. Located inside VIK’s titanium-roofed winery in Chile’s Cachapoal Valley, the restaurant was designed around sustainability and low-waste cooking. Menus change frequently based on produce from the estate gardens and local suppliers, while panoramic windows keep the surrounding hills and vineyards visible throughout the meal.

England

Back to Top

CHALK

Wiston Estate

Sussex, England

Chalk at Wiston Estate brings a distinctly English approach to wine-country dining. To pair with the sparkling wines they’re known for, you might get über-English dishes like mackerel with horseradish and elderflower, or Devonshire brill with samphire. Local game, meanwhile, might be paired with reds inspired by regions ranging from Australia to Burgundy.

THE RESTAURANT AT HAMBLEDON VINEYARD

Hambledon Vineyard

Hampshire, England

Hambledon is considered England’s oldest commercial vineyard, and its restaurant looks directly over the Hampshire vines that helped revive English sparkling wine. The atmosphere is country-estate, but the menu is proudly modern and seasonal. You may find guinea fowl, lamb sweetbreads, and chalk stream trout on the menu year-round, but paired with asparagus, peas, broccoli sprouts, and wild garlic in spring, and daikon, pickled onion, and chestnut in autumn.

France

Back to Top

CHEZ JEANNETTE

La Commanderie de Peyrassol

Provence, France

Chez Jeannette brings serious culinary ambition to the Provençal countryside, serving seasonal menus built around produce from the estate’s organic garden and nearby farms. Chef Benjamin Le Balch’s cooking remains elegant without losing the relaxed vineyard atmosphere, with dishes like lamb with coffee, beets, and poppy, or sériole (amberjack) with white asparagus and acacia flower vinegar. Long lunches in the glow of southern French light are the way to go.

LA GRAND’VIGNE

Château Smith Haut Lafitte

Pessac-Léognan, France

Located inside the Sources de Caudalie hotel, La Grand’Vigne sits beneath an elegant glass canopy inspired by 18th-century greenhouses, setting the stage for an exacting meal that borders on the theatrical. The two-Michelin-starred restaurant is known for polished French cuisine with a lighter touch; the six-course “Vegetal Terroir” menu is revelatory.

Georgia

Back to Top

CRAZY POMEGRANATE

Pheasant’s Tears

Kakheti, Georgia

Grab some of your favorite natural-wine pals for the custom feast here (reserved for groups of 10 or more), with dozens of Georgian dishes and rare qvevri-aged wines, overlooking vineyards at the base of the Caucasus Mountains. You’ll enjoy chvishtari, khachapuri, mtsvadi, and countless more vegetable-forward dishes, just 10 minutes from Sighnaghi, one of Georgia’s most lovely towns.

Germany

Back to Top

RESTAURANT SCHLOSSSCHÄNKE

Schloss Johannisberg

Rheingau, Germany

Schloss Johannisberg is one of Germany’s most historically significant wine estates, and Restaurant Schlossschänke serves Rheingau cuisine designed to complement the ripeness and sweetness levels of traditional Riesling. Guests dine inside historic castle spaces or on terraces overlooking the Rhine Valley and the dramatically steep Riesling vineyards descending toward the river.

Greece

Back to Top

ANHYDROUS CELLAR DOOR

Anhydrous Winery

Santorini, Greece

There’s nothing cookie-cutter about Anhydrous Winery or its restaurant; both embrace the intense, unique character of this hot, dry, volcanic region, battered by wind and ocean spray. Sweeping volcanic island views pair modern Greek dishes with Assyrtiko and other indigenous varieties, with a setting that feels at once ancient and ultramodern.

Hungary

Back to Top

PADI

Sauska

Tokaj, Hungary

Padi is a contemporary ode to Hungarian wine and Tokaj culinary tradition. Try salt-baked celery with Hungarian sturgeon caviar alongside a brisk Furmint, or Mangalitsa pork neck with cottage cheese dumplings and paprika sauce, paired with a surprisingly fresh and complex Pinot Noir. Save time for a cellar tour and a leisurely glass on the terrace.

BEST RESTAURANT DESIGN: Two intersecting lens-shaped discs, each 120 feet across, hover above a panorama of some of Hungary’s most exalted vineyards. Like a watchman’s eyes, the structure evokes the 24/7 guards who protect Hungary’s famed Holy Crown. Architect Péter Bordás of Hungary’s BORD Architectural Studio sought not to integrate the structure with nature but to stand in reverence of it. “What I create should not touch the untouchable,” he says. Built as much to accommodate Padi restaurant and bar as the winery itself, it’s a sublime ode to the great wine, hospitality, and dry humor that epitomize modern Hungary.

Italy

Back to Top

IL RISTORO DI CASTELLO DI AMA

Castello di Ama

Chianti, Italy

Housed in an 18th-century villa, Il Ristoro di Castello di Ama feels less like a standalone restaurant and more like an extension of Castello di Ama itself. Opt for the “Symposium” or “Feast” menus, which include intimate tours of the property, cellar, and the contemporary art installations scattered throughout. The kitchen focuses on traditional Tuscan cooking with a refined touch, while the quiet Chianti setting gives the experience an unhurried, almost private atmosphere.

INDIGENO

Salcheto Winery

Montepulciano, Italy

Indigeno reflects Salcheto’s longstanding focus on sustainable viticulture and low-impact production. The restaurant centers on seasonal ingredients that are regularly paired with Salcheto’s exceptional Sangioveses, produced at one of Italy’s best-known sustainable estates. The menu is closely connected to the surrounding landscapes, with salads of foraged greens, local game and deer, and breads and pastas made daily.

LA VEDUTA

Tenuta Cavalier Pepe

Campania, Italy

La Veduta makes the most of its position above the hills of Irpinia in Campania, focusing on regional dishes paired with estate wines made from Fiano, Greco, and Aglianico. Handmade pasta, slow-cooked meats, and produce from the surrounding countryside give the menu a distinctly local identity despite the polished winery setting.

LOCANDA COSTASERA AT MONTELEONE21

Masi Agricola

Valpolicella, Italy

There’s a “wine first” philosophy here, meaning that food is paired with the wine, not the other way around. Try tubetti pasta with peas, sumac, Roman mint, and goat cheese with a light-bodied natural Corvina-Merlot blend that provides a glittering backdrop of tart red fruit. The restaurant channels the warm, traditional side of Valpolicella hospitality, with stone walls, rustic interiors, and vineyard surroundings.

OSTERIA DI BROLIO

Barone Ricasoli

Chianti, Italy

Osteria di Brolio sits directly below Brolio Castle, surrounded by vineyards tied to some of Chianti Classico’s longest winemaking traditions. The menu combines Central Italian staples with a modern edge, like pappardelle al ragù, but with a duck ragù, wild fennel, and local olives. Meals tend to unfold slowly on the terrace, overlooking the hills around Gaiole.

RISTORANTE CAMPO DEL DRAGO

Castiglion del Bosco

Montalcino, Italy

Part of the expansive Castiglion del Bosco estate in Montalcino, where Brunello production and luxury hospitality are deeply intertwined, Ristorante Campo del Drago combines vineyard views with elegant Michelin-starred dining. While Brunello country usually sits somewhere between rustic charm and quiet luxury, Campo del Drago (and the broader atmosphere across the property) makes the case for the region as a luxe dining destination.

Japan

Back to Top

APERÇU

Niki Hills Winery

Hokkaido, Japan

Located in Hokkaido’s emerging wine region, your meal begins with a nature walk through the property’s fruit orchards and forests. The menu blends French technique with Hokkaido ingredients, like a papery rice wafer filled with foie gras and sweet bean paste, paired with a sparkling rosé made from Kerner, Bacchus, Cabernet Mitos, and Cabernet Dorsa grapes.

Liechtenstein

Back to Top

TORKEL

Liechtenstein Winery

Rheintal, Liechtenstein

Torkel combines fine dining with a sense of Liechtenstein history that few winery restaurants can match. The restaurant sits inside a former medieval wine building in the princely Herawingert vineyard north of Vaduz, owned by the Liechtenstein family since 1712. The menu moves between refined dishes like kingfish with grapefruit and bison filet, while guests drink wines produced from the vines directly outside the dining room.

Mexico

Back to Top

BÁRBARO ASADOR DE CAMP

Vinos Barrigones

Querétaro, Mexico

Bárbaro is an open-air, open-fire restaurant set among estate vineyards and a natural winery, where you can see the workers in action from your table. It’s worth the day trip from Mexico City, but you may end up staying longer for dishes like a taco of local Wagyu cross on an heirloom corn tortilla, crusted with griddled Oaxacan cheese and topped with avocado, chimichurri, and charred green chilies, and whole-fish “chicharrón,” torn into crispy chunks to dunk in nước chấm and tartar sauce.

LUNARIO

Vinícola La Lomita

Valle de Guadalupe, Mexico

Even in Valle de Guadalupe, a wine region well known for its culinary offerings, Lunario stands apart. Helmed by Chef Sheyla Alvarado, the tasting menu changes monthly and sources produce from the gardens at nearby sister winery and farm Finca La Carrodilla, and seafood like oysters, clams, abalone, sea urchin, and lobster from waters just 10 miles away. Delicate and painterly but never fussy, you may find oysters paired with kumquat, or smoked shrimp with bougainvillea.

Moldova

Back to Top

BUFNIȚA ALBĂ

Castel Mimi Winery

Bulboaca, Moldova

Bufnița Albă is modern Moldova at its best. The restaurant is part of one of the country’s best-known wine estates, originally established in 1893. Menus draw heavily on Moldovan culinary traditions, and the majestic castle architecture and formal landscaping lend a theatrical feel to the property.

New Zealand

Back to Top

AMISFIELD

Amisfield Winery

Central Otago, New Zealand

The setting is lovely—views of vineyards, Lake Hayes, and the Remarkables mountain range—but all eyes are on the plate. Amisfield is as much about terroir as it is about the winery. The tasting menu can reach 20 dishes, each listed by its central ingredients, most of which are native to New Zealand, such as wild pūtangitangi, tītī, mārari, and tāwaka. A bucket-list experience.

CRAGGY RANGE

Craggy Range Winery

Hawke’s Bay, New Zealand

Craggy Range offers one of New Zealand’s most imposing backdrops, with the jagged peak of Te Mata visible beyond the vineyards. The menu centers around ingredients from the estate’s organic culinary garden, with seafood featured prominently. A kumara (sweet potato) pancake comes with crayfish tartare, and pāua (abalone) is served with crumbed green-lipped mussels and Champagne sauce.

Portugal

Back to Top

BOMFIM 1896

Quinta do Bomfim

Douro, Portugal

Most restaurants say they’re inspired by old family traditions and classic hospitality, but few walk the walk like this restaurant from the iconic Port producer Symington. Set on the riverbanks in Pinhão, the restaurant centers its approach on wood fires, open kitchens, and recipes inspired by Douro home cooking, such as smoked eel and slow-roasted baby goat.

MALHADINHA NOVA

Herdade da Malhadinha Nova

Alentejo, Portugal

Malhadinha Nova feels less like a winery visit and more like staying at a stylish estate in the Alentejo region. The restaurant draws heavily from ingredients raised or grown on the property, including the coveted black Iberian pigs. Traditional Alentejo cooking appears throughout the menu, but is presented with a lighter, more contemporary touch that matches the estate’s fresh, modern wines and atmosphere of understated luxury.

MESA DE LEMOS

Quinta de Lemos

Dão, Portugal

The architecture of this restaurant follows the terrain of its hilltop location, with floor-to-ceiling glass that gives an alien vibe even as it seems to have grown out of the granite soil. Chef Diogo Rocha’s tasting menus epitomize modern Portuguese cooking, with each course focused on a single ingredient, like pandora fish from the Algarve, baby goat from Serra do Caramulo, or pineapple from the Azores.

South Africa

Back to Top

BEYOND

Buitenverwachting Wine Farm

Constantia, South Africa

Beyond at Buitenverwachting brings a modern edge to one of Constantia’s oldest wine estates. Chef Peter Tempelhoff offers a personal take on seasonal South African ingredients in his tasting menus, while the dining room overlooks the slopes of the Silvermine Mountain Range. The contrast between the historic Cape Dutch setting and the contemporary cooking creates a balance of tradition and innovation.

DELAIRE GRAFF RESTAURANT

Delaire Graff Estate

Stellenbosch, South Africa

Delaire Graff Estate leans unapologetically into luxury, but the setting draws much of the attention. Perched on Helshoogte Pass above Stellenbosch, the winery pairs vineyard views with South African art, sculpture, and dramatic architecture. The restaurant focuses on ingredients from the estate gardens and regional producers, morphed into artful dishes like raw springbok with fuzzy kei apple, or wagyu tongue with slaphakskeentjies (a sweet-and-sour onion dish), amasi (a fermented milk product), samphire, and rhubarb.

RUST EN VREDE RESTAURANT

Rust en Vrede

Stellenbosch, South Africa

Inspired by the owner’s visit to The French Laundry, Rust en Vrede has exemplified fine dining in Stellenbosch for nearly 20 years. The restaurant is housed in a 1780 Cape Dutch building that was once the historic estate’s cellar. Italian-Brazilian Chef Fabio Daniel brings global influences to the restaurant’s core concept of modern French cuisine, preparing rock lobster with coconut and apricot, for instance. Choose the “Experience” tasting menu, which is custom-designed for the diner and paired with the estate’s rarest bottles.

Spain

Back to Top

REFECTORIO

Abadía Retuerta

Ribera del Duero, Spain

Refectorio occupies a former 12th-century abbey dining hall, and few winery restaurants can match its sense of place. The Michelin-starred restaurant pairs Ribera del Duero wines with highly detailed tasting menus built around refined, almost austere, takes on traditional dishes that can feel practically medieval: pickled partridge, for example, or baby lamb in an eggplant stew called alboronía. Vaulted ceilings, candlelight, and monastery stone walls create a dramatic backdrop.

RESTAURANTE AMBIVIUM

Pago de Carraovejas

Ribera del Duero, Spain

Ambivium at Pago de Carraovejas was designed around the relationship between wine and food, not the other way around. The Michelin-starred restaurant offers tasting menus in which every course is connected to a specific wine pairing from the Pago de Carraovejas cellar. Dining rooms overlook the vineyards of Ribera del Duero, and the experience moves at a pensive pace that encourages guests to pay attention to every glass.

United States

Back to Top

AMBAR ESTATE

Ambar Estate Winery

Newberg, Oregon

The five-course tasting menu of local and organic ingredients—such as stinging nettle dumplings in clarified rhubarb broth—shares this Willamette Valley winery’s farming philosophies. Offered in concert with the winery visit, it nonetheless reads more as a restaurant than another wine-tasting experience. Don’t miss the burrata, paired with whatever fruit and herbs are in season, plus seasonings like crispy shallots, togarashi, and saba, made by reducing the estate’s grape must to a syrup.

THE BISCUIT

Los Milics Vineyards

Elgin, Arizona

This restaurant sits at the base of the Mustang Mountains in Arizona wine country and is named for the distinctive peak locals call “The Biscuit.” The architecture is striking, and the patio overlooks the high-desert grasslands of the Sonoita AVA. Chef Trevor Routman’s cooking keeps the experience grounded in the region, with dry-aged steak from Vera Earl Ranch, 15 minutes away, served with grilled oyster mushrooms and blackberry vinaigrette, paired with a Petit Verdot that exudes smoky dark-chocolate and foraged-berries notes.

BUBBLE ROOM

J Vineyards & Winery

Healdsburg, California

The titular “bubble” here refers as much to the custom cloud chandeliers as J’s acclaimed sparkling wines, since you’ll get plenty of still white and red wine with the five-course tasting menu. A recent menu included a local giant rock scallop with cherry salsa macha, apricot soubise, and the briny vegetable agretti, paired with a juicy Russian River Valley Estate Pinot Gris that has complementary notes of lemongrass and ginger.

CHATEAU MORRISETTE RESTAURANT

Chateau Morrisette

Floyd, Virginia

Chateau Morrisette Restaurant sits high along the Blue Ridge Parkway, where mountain views compete with the food for attention. Their farm-based southern Appalachian and French cuisine features ingredients sourced mostly from their own gardens and regional farms. Fireside dining in cooler months and terrace seating in warmer weather make it one of Virginia wine country’s most scenic year-round destinations.

CHEF’S COUNTER AT BURNT HILL FARM

Burnt Hill Farm

Clarksburg, Maryland

Not just one of the best winery restaurants but also one of the best new(ish) restaurants in the country, Chef’s Counter at Burnt Hill Farm is an intimate tasting counter seating just a handful of guests, with menus shaped by Chef Tae Strain’s Korean-influenced cooking. Ingredients come from the property’s 117-acre regenerative farm, and the experience is enlivened by Burnt Hill’s low-intervention wines.

NINER WINE ESTATES RESTAURANT

Niner Wine Estates

Paso Robles, California

The hospitality at Niner starts on “Heart Hill,” where century-old trees are planted in a heart shape. The food is equally romantic, sourced from a chef’s garden with over 300 edible crops in addition to their own honey and eggs; look for dishes seasoned with lavender, lemon verbena, and magnolia petals. While vegetable heavy, the menu often features unusual cuts like rib eye spinalis and pork secreto.

PALLADIO RESTAURANT

Barboursville Vineyards

Barboursville, Virginia

Palladio reflects the Italian roots of Barboursville Vineyards, which is known for Italian varieties like Sangiovese, Barbera, and Nebbiolo. But like the best Italian cuisine, it’s driven less by repetition and more by ingredients, many of which are grown on site. It results in inventive but no less comforting dishes like risotto of baby octopus and Malabar spinach, and meats like bison and elk.

SORRENTO’S RISTORANTE

Tsillan Cellars

Chelan, Washington

People tend to want vineyard views at a winery restaurant, but here all eyes are on gorgeous Lake Chelan, one of the most romantic wine regions and vacation areas in Washington State. Sorrento’s brings a little Italy to the area, with Tuscan columns on the building and dishes featuring the ingredient-focused cucina povera of Tuscany, using local meat and produce.

STOUT’S SIGNATURE AT GRAPE CREEK

Grape Creek Vineyards

Fredericksburg, Texas

The restaurant sits beside the vineyards at Grape Creek Vineyards along Highway 290, serving dishes like tenderloin bolognese and signature meatballs alongside estate wines. It also boasts the region’s best Sunday brunch, with tables laden with dishes like Bourbon shrimp and grits with homemade andouille sausage and a breakfast burger with egg, bacon, and avocado. A mini pancake stack for the table is a must. Pair it with their Cabernet Trois, a bold blend of Cab Sauvignon, Cab Franc, and Ruby Cabernet.

TABOR HILL RESTAURANT

Tabor Hill Winery

Buchanan, Michigan

Tabor Hill has been a southwest Michigan wine country institution for more than 50 years. Try the local walleye with Dijon cream and seasonal vegetables, or grilled chicken thighs over Asiago gnocchi napped with both white-wine velouté and basil-pistachio pesto. A dozen wines—dry and semi-sweet and fruit wines—pair remarkably well with the hearty fare.

THE KITCHEN AT ABEJA

Abeja Winery

Walla Walla, Washington

The restaurant sits on Abeja’s 49-acre farmstead, a short drive out of downtown Walla Walla, surrounded by vineyards, gardens, and wide open views. Executive Chef (and multiple James Beard Award nominee) Mike Easton’s Tuscan heart meets local ingredients like cherries, apples, pears, asparagus, sunchokes, corn, and an array of exotic potatoes.

THE RESTAURANT AT DELILLE CELLARS

DeLille Cellars

Woodinville, Washington

A short drive from Seattle, Woodinville is a hub of Washington wine, though much of it is served at warehouses where the wines, thankfully, outshine the industrial atmosphere. It’s why a meal at DeLille is so welcome, with sweeping vineyard views and food that screams “Western Washington,” like dandelion risotto with sunflower pistou and rhubarb, or Dungeness crab and corn pappardelle (order the double portion of crab).

VERAISONS RESTAURANT

Glenora Wine Cellars

Dundee, New York

Overlooking Seneca Lake in the heart of Finger Lakes wine country, Veraisons works closely with local farmers and changes its menus seasonally to highlight regional ingredients. The menu curiously combines Italian and pan-Asian ingredients in a way that somehow works brilliantly, like lemongrass chicken pot stickers followed by seafood garganelli with gremolata. Cathedral ceilings and a stone fireplace create a cozy atmosphere, while the terrace opens directly onto vineyard and lake views.

Uruguay

Back to Top

BODEGA GARZÓN RESTAURANT

Bodega Garzón

Maldonado, Uruguay

With Argentinian fire master Francis Mallmann as culinary director, wood-fire cooking rules at this obligatory stop in Uruguay wine country. Cocina rioplatense is the term for food that straddles both countries, but besides the fire-grilled meats that characterize their asados, Garzón’s tasting menus also feature fish and shrimp from Uruguay’s gorgeous coasts. The seemingly endless view passes over rocky vineyards to the ocean beyond.

RESTAURANT LAS ESPINAS

Bodega Bouza

Canelones, Uruguay

A short drive through Chardonnay, Tannat, Pinot Noir, and Merlot vineyards leads to this hilltop restaurant with a 360-degree view that overlooks vines on one side and the Atlantic Ocean on the other. The setting, combined with Uruguayan grilled beef and lamb, housemade cheeses and charcuterie, and a smattering of Spanish dishes like pulpo a la gallega and croquetas de jamón, makes it a restaurant where one can spend the whole day.

Letter from the Publisher

Italy, From the Mouths of Babes

Italian history captured my children’s attention faster than any gelato.

We recently took our five- and eight-year-old boys on their first trip to Italy, and it felt both like a privilege and a small act of parenting bravery.

Read More

My husband and I have always been ambitious, perhaps delusional, about traveling with our children. We have largely white-knuckled our way through international trips, forgoing sleep schedules, expensive childcare, and iPads (except on flights, when kids and adults are allowed unlimited screen time). Sure, my five-year-old colored all over the white comforter in our hotel room because he thought it would “look cool,” but everything mostly worked out fine.

When you picture kids in Italy, you probably imagine them ravenously eating pizza and gelato on ancient streets. And while there was plenty of that, we also taught them how to make pici, a hand-rolled Tuscan pasta I have only ever encountered in the region. They consumed so much prosciutto that they were buoyant with salt during their nightly bath.

But surprisingly, food was not their favorite part of the trip. What captured their attention more than the gelato was the history. Their minds were repeatedly blown by stories of Ancient Rome, gladiators, and just how old everything is. As American-born children (myself included, frankly), it is hard to grasp just how young the United States is in the timeline of global history. The scale of Europe bends your understanding of time in a way that feels almost impossible to explain until you witness it through a child’s eyes.

And that is the wonder of traveling with children: They notice what adults have long stopped seeing.

In Tuscany, we stayed at an agriturismo near Montepulciano, waking to rolling hills so beautiful they almost looked fake. But one of the most memorable moments of the trip was visiting Bindi Sergardi in Siena and spending time with its proprietor, Alessandra Casini Bindi Sergardi, and her sister, Maddalena. To explain Alessandra’s place in Siena’s history to my sons, I referred to her as the “Queen of Tuscany.” Disney lore is a useful shortcut when explaining aristocratic lineage to children. She’s the 23rd-generation steward of her family’s estate, so saying she’s in the “royal family of Siena” isn’t even much of an exaggeration.

Upon entering her home, my boys were fascinated by the ancient Etruscan architecture surrounding them. We kept trying to explain what “nearly 3,000 years old” actually means, and that these stones existed long before the United States, before Pokémon, and even before Ancient Rome and the gladiators. You could almost see their brains short-circuiting. And isn’t that what brings so many of us back to Italy again and again? The well of wonder never runs dry.

For this issue, we found ourselves once again captivated by Italy’s seemingly endless stories of history, food, and wine. There is always something left untold, another bottle to discover, another road not taken, another layer of history beneath your feet.

My boys reminded me of something I had begun taking for granted: Italy’s capacity for storytelling knows no bounds.

Italy’s Best-Kept Wine Travel Secret Is a Quick Jaunt from Venice

After landing at Venice’s Marco Polo Airport, why not head east instead? Postpone your gondola ride, rent a car, and cross into Friuli-Venezia Giulia, a lesser-visited region full of culinary gems.

Back From the Brink of Extinction

Wine

title: This Summery Matcha Gin Cocktail Comes from One of Brooklyn’s Coolest Rooftops
summary:
The Evergreen cocktail from Brooklyn’s Bar Blondeau is refreshing without being overly sweet and layered without feeling complicated.

The post This Summery Matcha Gin Cocktail Comes from One of Brooklyn’s Coolest Rooftops appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Jarett Karlsberg wanted to be an actor, but found his stage at Brooklyn’s Wythe Hotel in Williamsburg as beverage director for both Le Crocodile and Bar Blondeau, the French-inspired rooftop cocktail bar. At first glance, the transition from theater to hospitality might seem unexpected, but Karlsberg sees little difference between the two.

“I love making people feel happy, giving them new experiences they can take with them,” he says. “It’s really thrilling to introduce people to new spirits.” That philosophy shapes the drink menu at Bar Blondeau, where he and his team collaborate twice a year to build cocktails around seasonal ingredients and New York producers.

When it comes to designing a drink, Karlsberg compares the process to an actor learning their lines. “If you don’t know your specs, you’re not going to put on a good show,” he says. 

Take the Evergreen, an original cocktail created for Bar Blondeau’s seasonal menu that showcases the bar’s ingredient-first approach to drink making. Rather than relying on infusions, fat-washing, or other modern techniques, Karlsberg builds cocktails around thoughtfully selected spirits, allowing each ingredient to contribute its own distinct role.

“It’s very important to us that every cocktail has a beginning, middle, and end,” Karlsberg says. “You should be able to taste all the elements in some way.” The result is a drink that doesn’t fit neatly into any one cocktail category, but instead reveals itself in layers—from cooling botanicals and grassy matcha to bright citrus and a gently bittersweet finish.

A New Summer Cocktail Classic

The Evergreen cocktail began as an ode to a former colleague of the beverage team who passed away last summer. She was obsessed with matcha and always pushed the team to drink it, but Karlsberg wasn’t convinced it would make for a good cocktail. It was essential to create something that wouldn’t separate, would be shelf-stable, and delivered on the balance his team strives for. It took weeks to perfect, but its success has Karlsberg thinking the Evergreen will play a recurring role on the summer menu.  

As it should. At first sip, it tastes like a cucumber smoothie—something you’d seek post workout. As someone who doesn’t drink matcha, I’m incredibly surprised by how smooth and creamy it is, while also being both vegetal and fruit-forward without one note overpowering the other. It tastes deceptively healthy and therefore perfect for unwinding on a hot summer day. 

The grassy richness of ceremonial-grade matcha with cooling cucumber and watermelon gin, bright lemon, and delicate jasmine creates a freshness and vibrancy. Hints of strawberry and caramelized sweetness add depth, while a gentle bitter finish keeps every sip refreshing and balanced. Served over crushed ice and finished with a cucumber spear, the drink is refreshing without being overly sweet and layered without feeling complicated.

The Ingredients, As Explained by Karlsberg

Fort Hamilton New World Cucumber Watermelon Gin: “We chose Fort Hamilton New World Gin for its refreshing botanicals—cannonball watermelon and cucumber—which play wonderfully with the matcha.” 

Yoshi Matcha Liqueur: “We were looking for something made with real ceremonial-grade matcha that was delicious, wouldn’t separate too quickly in the cocktail, was shelf-stable, and wasn’t too polarizing. Yoshi ticked all those boxes.”

Matchbook Distilling Daytrip Strawberry Amaro: “Matchbook Distilling is an incredible local distillery that makes a wide range of truly impressive and unique spirits and liqueurs. Their Strawberry Amaro pairs beautifully with anything that has a slightly creamy texture (like matcha), and the touch of strawberry lifts all the other flavors.”

Muyu Jasmine Vert: “Made from yuzu, neroli, jasmine, and patchouli, a dash of Muyu Jasmine Vert adds floral and green aromatic notes to the cocktail.”

Cane Syrup: “Provides a touch of caramelized sweetness.”

Lemon Juice: “Fresh lemon juice provides the acidity needed to balance the other ingredients.”

The Evergreen Cocktail

Yield: 1

Ingredients

  • 1½ ounces Fort Hamilton New World Cucumber Watermelon Gin
  • ¾ ounce Yoshi Matcha Liqueur
  • ¼ ounce Matchbook Distilling Daytrip Strawberry Amaro
  • ¾ ounce fresh lemon juice
  • ¼ ounce cane syrup

Instructions

  1. Add all ingredients to a cocktail shaker with ice.
  2. Shake vigorously.
  3. Strain over crushed or pebble ice.
  4. Garnish with a cucumber spear.

Can You Substitute the Ingredients?

The Evergreen is an original recipe, but a few substitutions work well.

  • No cane syrup? Use simple syrup or honey syrup
  • Can’t find Fort Hamilton New World Gin? A cucumber-forward gin, such as Hendrick’s, makes a good substitute, though you’ll lose some of the watermelon character.
  • No Daytrip Strawberry Amaro? Dolin Chambéryzette or strawberry syrup can work, though both produce a sweeter cocktail.

What to Serve With It

Despite its health-food appearance, the Evergreen pairs with a surprisingly wide range of dishes. Karlsberg especially recommends:

  • Curry dishes
  • Spicy cuisine
  • Barbecue
  • Grilled meats
  • Roasted meats
  • Cheese boards

More Cocktail Coverage

  • Stir some sherry into the classic Paloma for an effortlessly delicious, low-proof cocktail.
  • This just in: Protein has come for cocktails.
  • Want to drink like a movie star? Shake up the official Oscars afterparty cocktails.
  • We’re begging you: Stop asking ChatGPT for drink recommendations.
